coredata.dc:title: The acetone sensing properties of ZnFe2O4-graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanocomposites at room temperature
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Chu, Xiangfeng
Dai, Peng
Liang, Shiming
Bhattacharya, Anish
Dong, Yongping
Epifani, Mauro
coredata.dc:description: ZnFe2O4/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanocomposites were prepared via hydrothermal method. The as-prepared nanocomposites were characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, HRTEM, TG, FTIR, Raman, XPS and N2 adsorption-desorption, respectively. The effect of GQDs content on the gas-sensing responses and the gas-sensing selectivity of the ZnFe2O4/GQDs nanocomposites was investigated. It was found that the sensor based on ZnFe2O4/GQDs nanocomposite (S-15) exhibited good response and good selectivity to acetone vapor at room temperature, the responses of the sensor based on ZnFe2O4/GQDs nanocomposite (S-15) to 1000 ppm acetone and 5 ppm acetone reached 13.3 and 1.2, respectively; the response times and the recovery times for 1000-5 ppm acetone were all shorter than 12 s. The ZnFe2O4/GQDs nanocomposite (S-15) will be a potential acetone sensing materials if its long-term gas sensing stability is improved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnFe2O4
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Acetone
Gas sensing properties

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD patterns of pure ZnFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites.
(a) The SEM image of pure ZnFe2O4 (S-0); (b) the SEM image of ZnFe2O4-GQDs (S-15); (c) the TEM images of ZnFe2O4-GQDs (S-15); (d, e) the HRTEM images of ZnFe2O4-GQDs (S-15).
The TGA curves of ZnFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites in air.
The Raman spectra of pure ZnFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4-GQDs.
The FT-IR spectra of ZnFe2O4 (S-0) and ZnFe2O4-GQDs composite (S-15).
XPS spectrum of (a) ZnFe2O4-GQDs (S-15); (b) Zn 2p; (c) C 1s; (d) Fe 2p.

(a) Nitrogen gas adsorption-desorption isotherms for pure ZnFe2O4 (S-0); (b) pore diameter distribution for ZnFe2O4 (S-0); (c) Nitrogen gas adsorption-desorption isotherms for ZnFe2O4-GQDs (S-15); (d) pore diameter distribution for ZnFe2O4-GQDs (S-15).
The responses to 1000 ppm acetone of the sensors based on S-0, S-5, S-10, S-15 and S-20 at different operating temperatures.
The responses of sensors based on S-0 and S-15 to different gases at room temperature.
The response transients of the sensor based on sample S-15 composite to acetone (1000, 500, 250, 100, 10 and 5 ppm) at room temperature.
The acetone sensing performances of the materials reported in literature and this work.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: “-”means that there is no data in the literature.

head.ce:title: The acetone sensing properties of ZnFe2O4-graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanocomposites at room temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243002, Anhui, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Linyi University, Linyi 276005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Consiglio Nazionale delle Ricerche, Istituto per la Microelettronica ed i Microsistemi, via Monteroni, I-73100 Lecce, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Istituto per la Microelettronica ed i Microsistemi

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ZnFe2O4/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanocomposites were prepared via hydrothermal method. The as-prepared nanocomposites were characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, HRTEM, TG, FTIR, Raman, XPS and N2 adsorption-desorption, respectively. The effect of GQDs content on the gas-sensing responses and the gas-sensing selectivity of the ZnFe2O4/GQDs nanocomposites was investigated. It was found that the sensor based on ZnFe2O4/GQDs nanocomposite (S-15) exhibited good response and good selectivity to acetone vapor at room temperature, the responses of the sensor based on ZnFe2O4/GQDs nanocomposite (S-15) to 1000 ppm acetone and 5 ppm acetone reached 13.3 and 1.2, respectively; the response times and the recovery times for 1000-5 ppm acetone were all shorter than 12 s. The ZnFe2O4/GQDs nanocomposite (S-15) will be a potential acetone sensing materials if its long-term gas sensing stability is improved.

ce:section.ce:para: Acetone is widely applied in many fields of industrial production. It is reported that headache, fatigue and harm to nerve system and visceral organs will be caused when the concentration of acetone in inhalation is higher than 173 ppm [1–3]. Medical investigations have shown that the acetone concentration in exhaled breath from a healthy human body is lower than 0.8 ppm, but that for a diabetic patient is higher than 1.8 ppm [4–7]. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop gas sensor for accurate analysis of acetone gas in breath and environment.

Metal oxide semiconductor based sensors have been under extensive investigations duo to their high sensitivity, fast response, simple fabrication and low cost [8–12]. Many metal oxides or doped metal oxides, such as SnO2 [10,11], doped SnO2 [12–17], ZnO [18–20], doped ZnO [21,22], TiO2 [23,24], have been reported to exhibit high sensitivity to acetone, but the optimum operating temperatures were all higher than 200 °C. The gas sensing properties of ZnFe2O4 have attracted much attention in recent years; the sensitivity and the selectivity are affected by preparation method and preparation conditions. ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles with diameter of about 10 nm exhibited enhanced gas sensing performance to acetone, the response value reached 39.5 for 200 ppm acetone gas at lower optimum working temperature of 200 °C and the detection limit was about 5 ppm [25]. The obtained ZnFe2O4 films obtained by spray pyrolysis showed high response to ethanol at concentrations as low as 1 ppm and fast response–recovery behavior at 390 °C [26]. Porous ZnFe2O4 sensor exhibited excellent selectivity toward acetone at the operating temperature 200 °C and the detection limit could reach 800 ppb [27]. ZnFe2O4 yolk–shell microsphere sensor showed an extremely high response and a low detection limit to acetone at the operating temperature of 200 °C [28]. ZnFe2O4 ZnO hybrid showed enhanced responses compared with the pure ZnO sensor [29–32], which indicated that doping could improve the gas sensing performances of ZnFe2O4.

Graphene (G) has been considered as promising candidates for sensing materials that can detect extremely low concentrations of gases such as CO2 [33], NH3 [34], H2 [35], trimethlyamine (TMA) [36]. The existence of graphene in the composites of graphene-metal oxides facilitated the electron transfer and electrical conductivity, the composite sensors showed improved performance at a lower temperature comparing with that of the corresponding bare metal oxides [37–42]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are graphene sheets smaller than 100 nm, which present unique optical and electronic properties due to their quantum confinement and edge effects. In our previous work [43], GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composites were prepared by loading GQDs with α-Fe2O3 via a one-step facile hydrothermal method, GQDs improved the gas sensing response and gas sensing selectivity to TMA and the detection limit of the optimum GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composite for TMA reached 0.01 ppm.

In this paper, we prepared ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites and investigated the gas sensing performances of ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites. It was found that the ZnFe2O4-GQDs composite with optimum composition exhibited high response and high selectivity to acetone at room temperature.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared through hydrothermal method which was similar to that reported in literature [44–46]. 2.5 g of citric acid was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water, and stirred for 30 min; the clear citric acid solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C in an oven for 5 h. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) suspension was obtained after the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature.

ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites were prepared by hydrothermal method. The typical synthesis process was as follows: firstly, different amounts of GQDs suspension (0 mL, 5 mL, 10 mL, 15 mL and 20 mL) was diluted with deionized water until the volume was 40 mL, 1.485 g of analytically pure Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 3.420 g of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O was dissolved in the diluted suspension under vigorous stirring for 30 min, and the mixed suspension was sonicated for 10 min; secondly, 1 mol/L NaOH solution was dropped slowly into the mixed suspension until pH value was 11.0 under stirring for 30 min; finally, the above mixture was transferred into 100 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 10 h. After the autoclave was cooled down to the room temperature naturally, the obtained products were filtered, washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol for several times, and dried at 80 °C for 24 h. The samples were correspondingly named as S-0, S-5, S-10, S-15 and S-20, respectively.

The crystal structures of ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 advance) using Cu-Ka (λ = 0.15418 nm) radiation at 40 kV and 30 mA in a 2θ range from 10° to 80° at room temperature. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were taken on a Hitachi S-4800 microscope, operating at 10 kV. Transmission electron micrographs (TEM) were obtained with high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN). Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) was carried out using a Netzsch STA449F3 system at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. Surface bonding and functional groupings of the composites were studied by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy using a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer in the range 400–4000 cm−1, with the KBr pellet technique. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-3600 spectrometer, BaSO4 was taken as a reference sample, the wavelength range is 200–800 nm. Raman spectra were acquired on a Renishaw Invia Raman Microprobe, the excitation wavelength is 532 nm and the scanning range is 3200–100 cm−1. The specific surface areas were analyzed by BET method through nitrogen adsorption–desorption measurement using an ASAP2010C instrument. The chemical surface's valence states of elements were performed using Thermo ESCALAB250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with all of the binding energy corrected by contaminant carbon (C1s = 284.6 eV).

A slurry composed of ZnFe2O4-GQDs and terpineol was coated onto the surface of a prefabricated alumina tube with a small brush, and dried at 80 °C for 10 h in a vacuum oven to remove terpineol. A NiCr alloy wire inserted in the alumina tube was used to control the operating temperature in the range of 20–450 °C. The response of the sensor was defined as the ratio of the stable electrical resistance of gas sensor in air (Ra) to that in the test gases (Rg). The measurement of gas sensing properties of the sensors was same as that in our previous work [47], the relative humidity in the base gas and the detected gases was about 50%. The response time and recovery time were defined as the time for a sensor to reach 90% value of the final signal.

Fig. 1 showed the XRD patterns of pure ZnFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites. The peaks at 2θ of 29.96°, 35.16°, 42.89°, 53.35°, 56.88° and 62.36° in the XRD pattern of pure ZnFe2O4 sample matched with (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) crystal planes of spinel ZnFe2O4 (JCPDS 22-1012); it could be found that the intensities of the peaks of ZnFe2O4 in the XRD patterns of ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites decreased with the GQDs content in ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites decreasing, which may result from the exfoliation of ZnFe2O4 by decorating GQDs [48]; the mean sizes of ZnFe2O4 particles in pure ZnFe2O4 (S-0), S-5, S-10, S-15 and S-20 composites estimated from Scherrer formula were 25, 20, 14, 12 and 9 nm, respectively; the decrease of ZnFe2O4 in the composites was mainly result from the confinement of GQDs [49]. The diffraction peaks of GQDs could not be found in all XRD patterns of ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites, which might be ascribed to the low diffraction intensity of GQDs, the screening effect of ZnFe2O4 diffraction peaks on that of GQDs [50] and low content of GQDs in the composite [51].

The morphology and sizes of the as-prepared pure ZnFe2O4 and S-15 were characterized by SEM, TEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1(a), there were a lot of aggregates with diameters in the range of 50–250 nm that were composed of smaller particles in pure ZnFe2O4 sample; Fig. 2 (b, c) indicated that the sizes of the agglomerates in S-15 were in the range of 20–50 nm, the agglomerates were also composed of smaller particles. The addition of GQDs in ZnFe2O4-GQDs composite (S-15) impeded the agglomeration of ZnFe2O4 crystals, this was also in agreement with the hypothesis that GQDs contributed to the growth of small grains, as seen in XRD patterns. The HRTEM images of S-15 were shown in Fig. 2 (d, e), the lattice spacing of the (104) of graphitic carbon (about 0.194 nm) [45] and the (220) of spinel ZnFe2O4 (0.298 nm) were clearly observed. These results affirmed that there were ZnFe2O4 and GQDs in the as-prepared composite.

The contents of GQDs in ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites were determined by TG analysis. As shown in Fig. 3 , the TGA curve of pure ZnFe2O4 exhibited a downward slope, which might result from normal instrument drift and slow desorption of water in the very small pores of ZnFe2O4 materials, because G. Zhang et al. [52] found that ZnFe2O4 did not have weight loss in the range of 400–800 °C. For ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites, the weight loss below 300 °C could be ascribed to desorption of adsorbed water, desorption of water formed from the condensation reaction of hydroxyls on the edges of GQDs. The significant fast drops in mass in the temperature range of 300–500 °C was attributed to the reaction between GQDs and oxygen in air. The GQDs contents of S-5, S-10, S-15 and S-20 estimated from TGA curves were 3%, 6%, 9% and 11%, respectively.

Raman spectroscopy was a powerful and widely used technique to confirm the existence of GQDs in the composite. The Raman spectra of pure ZnFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4-GQDs were shown in Fig. 4 , there were three peaks at 355 cm−1, 480 cm−1 and 650 cm−1 in the Raman spectra of pure ZnFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4-GQDs, which could be ascribed to spinel ZnFe2O4 vibration mode [53]. The Raman spectrum of the as-prepared ZnFe2O4-GQDs with a G band at 1601 cm−1, D band at 1380 cm−1 confirmed the presence of defects in case of the as-prepared ZnFe2O4-GQDs [54,55].

Fig. 5 depicted the FT-IR spectra of ZnFe2O4 (S-0) and ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites (S-15). The absorption bands at high frequency 3430 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1 could be observed obviously, which indicated the existence of OH bonds of adsorbed water molecules on the surface of ZnFe2O4 (S-0) and ZnFe2O4-GQDs [56]. For pure ZnFe2O4, the two absorption peaks at lower frequency (around 571 and 479 cm−1) could be assigned to the stretching vibrations of the ZnO bonds in tetrahedral positions and the FeO bonds in octahedral positions; in the FTIR spectrum of ZnFe2O4/GQDs (S-15), the characteristic bands of ZnO and FeO shifted to 554 and 453 cm−1, which resulted from the formation of the ZnFe2O4-GQDs nanohybrid [48]. The peaks located at 1723 cm−1, 1399 cm−1 and 1070 cm−1 could be attributed to characteristic bands of the CO stretching vibrations of COOH groups, the stretching vibration of CO (carboxyl) and the stretching vibration of CO (alkoxy), respectively [48,56,57], which further confirmed the presence of GQDs in ZnFe2O4-GQDs composite (S-15).

The composition and the chemical state of the elements of ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites (S-15) were characterized by XPS. Fig. 6 (a) showed that ZnFe2O4-GQDs composite (S-15) was clearly composed of Zn, Fe, O and C. Two fitting peaks with binding energy values of 1045.0 and 1022.1 eV exhibited in the Zn 2p spectrum in Fig. 6 (b) could be attributed to Zn 2p 1/2 and Zn 2p 3/2, respectively [58]; C 1s spectrum shown in Fig. 6(c) exhibited three peaks at 284.9, 285.7 and 288.8 eV, which were attributed to the CC, CC and OCO bands in GQDs-based materials [40]. These XPS results indicated that ZnFe2O4-GQDs composite had formed in the process of hydrothermal reaction; as shown in Fig. 6(d), the Fe 2p spectrum consisted of Fe 2p 3/2 and Fe 2p 1/2, the binding energy peaks for Fe 2p3/2 at 713.6 and 711.5 eV corresponded well with the tetrahedral site (A site) and octahedral (B site), respectively [58], the peaks occurred at the binding energy of 725.6 eV and 719.3 eV were well consistent with Fe 2p1/2 [59].

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of ZnFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4-GQDs (S-15) composite were shown in Fig. 7 . The two isotherms were typical IV type adsorption-desorption isotherms, which indicated existence of meso-porous structure; the average pore size for ZnFe2O4 was 13.4 nm, but the average pore size for ZnFe2O4-GQDs decreased to 2.5 nm; the BET specific areas for S-0, S-5, S-10, S-15 and S-20 were 31.1, 81.1, 180.9, 214.8 and 124.7 m2 g−1, respectively.

Fig. 8 showed the responses to 1000 ppm acetone of the sensors based on S-0, S-5, S-10, S-15 and S-20. The responses of all sensors to 1000 ppm were very low when the operating temperatures were higher than 50 °C. The response of gas sensing materials was closely related to the preparation method and preparation conditions of the materials. The response {S (%) = [(Ra-Rg)/Ra] × 100%} of pure ZnFe2O4 prepared by glycine combustion method attained maximum value when operating at 250 °C, the response to 2000 ppm acetone was 57% [60]. The nanosheet-assembled ZnFe2O4 hollow microspheres prepared by solvothermal method combined with the subsequent thermal treatment exhibited high response to acetone at the operating temperature of 215 °C, the response to 200 ppm acetone reached 47 [61]. The response to 1000 ppm acetone of pure ZnFe2O4 in this paper reached a maximum value at 310 °C, the response to 1000 ppm acetone was only 1.9. The responses of the ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites to 1000 ppm acetone were all higher than that of S-0 at room temperature, the response of the sensor based on S-15 (optimum composition) to 1000 ppm acetone attained 13.3 at room temperature. The improvement of graphene on gas sensing properties has been reported by many researchers [62]. GQDs are graphene with smaller sizes, the good gas sensing properties of ZnFe2O4-GQDs may be ascribed to the following factors: firstly, GQDs could enhance the conductivity of the sensors of ZnFe2O4-GQDs comparing with pure ZnFe2O4; secondly, the specific areas of ZnFe2O4-GQDs composites were larger than that of pure ZnFe2O4, which was revealed by the N2 adsorption-desorption experimental results; thirdly, the improvement of gas sensing properties was related to the interaction between ZnFe2O4 and GQDs. In ZnFe2O4-GQDs composite, a heterojunction and a depletion zone may form at the interface, which facilitated the detection of acetone molecule through the resistance variation of ZnFe2O4-GQDs composite. The attachment of ZnFe2O4 onto GQDs also resulted in more active sites for acetone molecules. The sensing mechanism of ZnFe2O4-GQDs composite was similar to that of ZnFe2O4 [58], which based on the resistance variation of the sensor by the adsorption and reaction process of oxygen molecules on the surface of material. Oxygen molecules adsorb on the surface of ZnFe2O4-GQDs and captured free electrons from the conduction band of ZnFe2O4 and formed adsorbed oxygen species, which led to the decrease of electrons concentration and the increase of the sensor resistance. When the sensor exposed to acetone, the acetone molecules reacted with the adsorbed oxygen species, the reaction equation was following:
C3H6O (ads) + 4 O2 −(ads) = 3CO2 + 3H2O + 4 e

This reaction gave the captured electrons back to the conduction band of ZnFe2O4, which lowered resistance of the sensor. The responses of the sensors based on S-0 and S-15 to different gases at room temperature were depicted in Fig. 9 , the responses of the sensor based S-0 to 1000 ppm acetic acid, acetaldehyde, ethanol, acetone trimethylamine, and ammonia were 1.2, 1.2, 1.0, 1.1, 1.3 and 1.3, respectively; but the responses of the sensor based on S-15 to 1000 ppm acetic acid, acetaldehyde, ethanol, acetone, trimethylamine and ammonia were 6.7, 5.3, 2.2, 13.3, 7.9 and 6.4, respectively. It has been reported that La0.9Sr0.1CoO3 nanoparticles showed good acetone and ethanol sensing performances at room temperature in the concentration range of 10–100 ppm [63]. Three-dimensional grass-like carbon-doped ZrO2 film exhibited high response to acetone, methanol and ethanol at room temperature, the detection was about 10 ppm [64]. The acetone sensing performances of the materials reported in some literature and this work were listed in Table 1 , as shown in Table 1, the ratio of the response to 1000 ppm acetone of S-15 to that to 1000 ppm ethanol attained 13.3/2.2 = 6, which indicated that the selectivity to acetone was increased greatly.

Response-recovery characteristics were very important parameters of the gas sensor. In general, the response time and recovery time are defined as the time for a sensor to reach 90% of the final signal. The response transients of the sensor based on sample S-15 composite to acetone (1000, 500, 250, 100, 10 and 5 ppm) at room temperature were shown in Fig. 10 . The responses to 1000, 500, 250, 100, 10 and 5 ppm acetone were 13.3, 10.7, 5.5, 3.2 and 1.2, respectively; the minimum detection limit of the sensor based on sample S-15 composite for acetone was 5 ppm. The response time and recovery time for 1000, 500, 250, 100, 10 and 5 ppm acetone were 9, 5, 6, 11, 5 and 4 s, respectively; the response time and recovery time for 1000, 500, 250, 100, 10 and 5 ppm acetone were 4, 3, 3, 4, 3 and 2 s, respectively. The gas sensing stability was also an important index for a practical gas sensor, the stability of the sensor based on S-15 was not ideal because its gas sensing response decreased obviously after one week, the investigation on improvement of the stability is on the way in our group. ZnFe2O4-GQDs composite (S-15) are a candidate for room temperature acetone sensing materials if its gas sensing stability is improved.

ZnFe2O4-graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanocomposites were prepared via hydrothermal method. GQDs have a great influence over the gas-sensing responses and the gas-sensing selectivity of the ZnFe2O4-GQDs nanocomposites. It was found that the sensor based on ZnFe2O4-GQDs nanocomposite (S-15) exhibited good response and good selectivity to acetone vapor at room temperature, the responses of the sensor based on ZnFe2O4-GQDs nanocomposite (S-15) to 1000 ppm acetone and 5 ppm acetone reached 13.3 and 1.2, respectively; the response time and the recovery time for 1000-5 ppm acetone were all very short. The ZnFe2O4-GQDs nanocomposite (S-15) is a potential acetone sensing materials if its long term gas sensing stability is improved.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by NSFC (No. 61671019).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.physe.2018.08.003.
